URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deep_learning

Deep Learning

Deep learning is part of a broader family of machine learning methods, which is based on artificial neural networks with representation learning. The adjective "deep" in deep learning refers to the use of multiple layers in the network. Methods used can be either supervised, semi-supervised or unsupervised.

tiple layers in the network. Methods used can be either supervised, semi-supervised or unsupervised.

Deep-learning architectures such as deep neural networks, recurrent neural networks (RNNs), convolutional neural networks (CNNs), long short-term memory (LSTM), transformers, and others have been applied to fields including computer vision, speech recognition, natural language processing, machine translation, bioinformatics, drug design, medical image analysis, climate science, material inspecti

translation, bioinformatics, drug design, medical image analysis, climate science, material inspection and board game programs.

History

The first general, working learning algorithm for supervised deep feedforward multilayer perceptrons was published by Alexey Ivakhnenko and Lapa in 1967. A 1971 paper described a deep network with eight layers trained by the group method of data handling algorithm.

r described a deep network with eight layers trained by the group method of data handling algorithm.

Other deep learning working architectures, specifically those built for computer vision, began with the Neocognitron introduced by Kunihiko Fukushima in 1980.

In 1989, Yann LeCun and colleagues applied the standard backpropagation algorithm to a deep neural network with the purpose of recognizing handwritten zip code numbers provided by the US Postal Service.

work with the purpose of recognizing handwritten zip code numbers provided by the US Postal Service.

The vanishing gradient problem was identified by Sepp Hochreiter in 1991 and his advisor Jürgen Schmidhuber in 1992. This problem affected training of deep networks, as gradients became very small during backpropagation.

s problem affected training of deep networks, as gradients became very small during backpropagation.

In 1997, Hochreiter and Schmidhuber proposed long short-term memory (LSTM), which overcame the vanishing gradient problem for recurrent networks. LSTM became widely used for sequence-to-sequence tasks.

ing gradient problem for recurrent networks. LSTM became widely used for sequence-to-sequence tasks.

The breakthrough moment came in 2012 when AlexNet, a convolutional neural network designed by Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton, won the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC) with top-5 error rates dramatically better than non-deep approaches.

Neural Network Architectures

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

better than non-deep approaches.

Neural Network Architectures

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

A convolutional neural network (CNN) is a type of artificial neural network used primarily for image recognition, classification, and analysis. CNNs are distinguished from other neural networks by their superior performance with image, speech, or audio signal inputs.

from other neural networks by their superior performance with image, speech, or audio signal inputs.

A CNN typically consists of:
- Convolutional layers that apply filters to detect features
- Pooling layers that reduce spatial dimensions
- Fully connected layers for classification
- Activation functions like ReLU

Famous CNN architectures include LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, GoogLeNet/Inception, ResNet, and EfficientNet.

Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

eNet, AlexNet, VGG, GoogLeNet/Inception, ResNet, and EfficientNet.

Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

A recurrent neural network (RNN) is a class of artificial neural networks where connections between nodes can create a cycle, allowing output from some nodes to affect subsequent input to the same nodes. This allows it to exhibit temporal dynamic behavior.

s to affect subsequent input to the same nodes. This allows it to exhibit temporal dynamic behavior.

RNNs can use their internal state (memory) to process variable-length sequences of inputs, making them applicable to tasks such as unsegmented, connected handwriting recognition, speech recognition, and natural language processing.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

ing recognition, speech recognition, and natural language processing.

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)

Long short-term memory (LSTM) networks are a type of recurrent neural network capable of learning long-term dependencies. They were introduced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997 and have been refined and popularized in subsequent work.

uced by Hochreiter and Schmidhuber in 1997 and have been refined and popularized in subsequent work.

LSTM units are composed of a cell, an input gate, an output gate and a forget gate. The cell remembers values over arbitrary time intervals and the three gates regulate the flow of information into and out of the cell.

Transformer Architecture

the three gates regulate the flow of information into and out of the cell.

Transformer Architecture

The transformer is a deep learning architecture introduced in the paper "Attention Is All You Need" by Vaswani et al. in 2017. It relies entirely on self-attention mechanisms to draw global dependencies between input and output.

t relies entirely on self-attention mechanisms to draw global dependencies between input and output.

Transformers have become the dominant architecture in natural language processing. They are the basis for large language models (LLMs) such as GPT, BERT, T5, and many others.

The key innovation of transformers is the attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different parts of the input when producing each part of the output.

odel to weigh the importance of different parts of the input when producing each part of the output.

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)

A generative adversarial network (GAN) is a class of machine learning frameworks designed by Ian Goodfellow and his colleagues in June 2014. Two neural networks contest with each other in the form of a zero-sum game, where one agent's gain is another agent's loss.

test with each other in the form of a zero-sum game, where one agent's gain is another agent's loss.

Given a training set, this technique learns to generate new data with the same statistics as the training set. For example, a GAN trained on photographs can generate new photographs that look at least superficially authentic to human observers.

Autoencoders

enerate new photographs that look at least superficially authentic to human observers.

Autoencoders

An autoencoder is a type of artificial neural network used to learn efficient codings of unlabeled data (unsupervised learning). An autoencoder learns two functions: an encoding function that transforms the input data, and a decoding function that recreates the input data from the encoded representation.

e input data, and a decoding function that recreates the input data from the encoded representation.

Variational autoencoders (VAEs) are a probabilistic variant that learn a latent space representation and can be used to generate new data.

Training Deep Networks

Activation Functions

e representation and can be used to generate new data.

Training Deep Networks

Activation Functions

Activation functions determine the output of a neural network node given an input or set of inputs. Common activation functions include:
- Sigmoid: Maps values to range (0,1), used for binary classification
- ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): max(0,x), helps solve vanishing gradient problem
- Tanh: Maps values to range (-1,1)
- Softmax: Used in output layer for multi-class classification
- GELU: Use

ps values to range (-1,1)
- Softmax: Used in output layer for multi-class classification
- GELU: Used in transformer models like BERT and GPT

Optimization Algorithms

Gradient descent is the primary optimization algorithm for training neural networks.

ion Algorithms

Gradient descent is the primary optimization algorithm for training neural networks. Variants include:
- Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): Updates weights using one sample at a time
- Mini-batch Gradient Descent: Uses small batches of data
- Adam: Adaptive learning rate optimizer, widely used in practice
- RMSprop: Adapts learning rates based on recent gradient magnitudes

Regularization

sed in practice
- RMSprop: Adapts learning rates based on recent gradient magnitudes

Regularization

To prevent overfitting in deep networks:
- Dropout: Randomly deactivates neurons during training
- Batch Normalization: Normalizes layer inputs to improve training stability
- Weight Decay (L2 regularization): Penalizes large weights
- Data Augmentation: Artificially increases training set size

Applications of Deep Learning

weights
- Data Augmentation: Artificially increases training set size

Applications of Deep Learning

Deep learning has revolutionized many fields:
- Image recognition and object detection
- Natural language processing and machine translation
- Speech recognition and synthesis
- Drug discovery and protein structure prediction (AlphaFold)
- Autonomous vehicles
- Game playing (AlphaGo, AlphaZero)
- Art and music generation

Large Language Models

A large language model (LLM) is a type of machine learning model designed for natural language processing tasks such as language generation. As language models, LLMs acquire these abilities by learning statistical relationships from vast amounts of text during a self-supervised and semi-supervised training process.

lationships from vast amounts of text during a self-supervised and semi-supervised training process.

The largest and most capable LLMs are artificial general intelligence (AGI) candidates, in the sense that they are able to perform a wide variety of tasks.

Overview

ence (AGI) candidates, in the sense that they are able to perform a wide variety of tasks.

Overview

Large language models have emerged as a transformative technology in artificial intelligence. They are trained on massive text corpora—often hundreds of billions of tokens—and contain billions of parameters. This combination of scale gives them impressive abilities in language understanding, generation, reasoning, and more.

of scale gives them impressive abilities in language understanding, generation, reasoning, and more.

LLMs are based on the transformer architecture, introduced in 2017. The key innovation is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to consider the full context of the input when generating each output token.

Architecture

Transformer Architecture

Modern LLMs use variations of the original transformer architecture:

ture

Transformer Architecture

Modern LLMs use variations of the original transformer architecture:

Encoder-only models (e.g., BERT):
- Process input bidirectionally
- Suitable for understanding tasks (classification, NER, QA)
- Trained with masked language modeling (MLM)

Decoder-only models (e.g., GPT):
- Process input autoregressively (left to right)
- Suitable for generation tasks
- Trained with causal language modeling (CLM)

sively (left to right)
- Suitable for generation tasks
- Trained with causal language modeling (CLM)

Encoder-decoder models (e.g., T5, BART):
- Combine encoder for understanding and decoder for generation
- Suitable for translation, summarization

Attention Mechanism

erstanding and decoder for generation
- Suitable for translation, summarization

Attention Mechanism

The attention mechanism computes a weighted sum of values, where weights are determined by the similarity between a query and a set of keys. Multi-head attention applies multiple attention operations in parallel, allowing the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces.

ns in parallel, allowing the model to attend to information from different representation subspaces.

Self-attention: Query, Key, and Value all come from the same sequence
Cross-attention: Query from one sequence, Key and Value from another

Training

Pre-training

LLMs are first pre-trained on massive text corpora using self-supervised objectives:
- Causal Language Modeling: Predict the next token given previous tokens
- Masked Language Modeling: Predict masked tokens from context

the next token given previous tokens
- Masked Language Modeling: Predict masked tokens from context

Pre-training datasets include:
- CommonCrawl: Web crawl data
- Books and Wikipedia
- GitHub code
- Academic papers (Arxiv)
- Social media (Reddit, StackExchange)

Fine-tuning

ikipedia
- GitHub code
- Academic papers (Arxiv)
- Social media (Reddit, StackExchange)

Fine-tuning

After pre-training, LLMs can be fine-tuned for specific tasks:
- Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): Train on curated instruction-following data
- Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF): Use human preferences to guide learning
- Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): Alternative to RLHF

Scaling Laws

ferences to guide learning
- Direct Preference Optimization (DPO): Alternative to RLHF

Scaling Laws

Research by Kaplan et al. (2020) at OpenAI showed that model performance scales predictably with compute, data, and parameter count according to power laws. The Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al., 2022) revised these laws to suggest models should be trained on more data relative to their size.

Notable Large Language Models

suggest models should be trained on more data relative to their size.

- GPT-3 (2020): 175B parameters, demonstrated few-shot learning
- InstructGPT (2022): Fine-tuned with RLHF
- GPT-4 (2023): Multimodal, significantly improved reasoning

nstructGPT (2022): Fine-tuned with RLHF
- GPT-4 (2023): Multimodal, significantly improved reasoning

- RoBERTa: Optimized BERT training
- DistilBERT: Lightweight BERT
- ALBERT: Parameter-efficient BERT

Capabilities and Limitations

e helpful, harmless, and honest

Capabilities and Limitations

Capabilities
- Text generation and completion
- Question answering
- Summarization and translation
- Code generation and debugging
- Mathematical reasoning
- Few-shot and zero-shot learning

gging

Limitations and Challenges
- Hallucination: Generating factually incorrect information confidently
- Knowledge cutoff: Training data has a fixed end date
- Context length: Limited context window size
- Bias: Reflects biases present in training data
- Computational cost: Expensive to train and run
- Interpretability: Difficult to understand internal reasoning

Hallucination

nsive to train and run
- Interpretability: Difficult to understand internal reasoning

Hallucination

LLM hallucination refers to the phenomenon where LLMs generate text that is factually incorrect, nonsensical, or unfaithful to the provided context. This remains one of the most significant challenges in LLM deployment.

hful to the provided context. This remains one of the most significant challenges in LLM deployment.

Approaches to reduce hallucination:
- Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG): Ground responses in retrieved documents
- Chain-of-thought prompting: Encourage step-by-step reasoning
- Constitutional AI: Use self-critique to improve responses
- Calibration: Make models express uncertainty appropriately

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

s
- Calibration: Make models express uncertainty appropriately

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

RAG is a technique that augments LLMs with external knowledge retrieval. The process:
1. Index a knowledge base with dense vector embeddings
2. When a query is received, encode it with the same embedding model
3. Retrieve the top-k most similar documents using cosine similarity or dot product
4. Include retrieved documents in the LLM's context
5.

uments using cosine similarity or dot product
4. Include retrieved documents in the LLM's context
5. Generate an answer grounded in the retrieved information

RAG systems typically use vector databases such as FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, or Pinecone for efficient similarity search.

Embeddings for RAG

es such as FAISS, Chroma, Weaviate, or Pinecone for efficient similarity search.

Embeddings for RAG

Common embedding models used in RAG:
- OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002: Popular, high-quality
- Sentence Transformers: Open-source, various sizes
- all-MiniLM-L6-v2: Fast and efficient
- INSTRUCTOR: Instruction-following embeddings
- BGE models: State-of-the-art open-source embeddings

Evaluation of LLMs

ction-following embeddings
- BGE models: State-of-the-art open-source embeddings

Evaluation of LLMs

LLMs are evaluated on standardized benchmarks:
- MMLU: Massive Multitask Language Understanding
- HellaSwag: Commonsense reasoning
- HumanEval: Code generation
- GSM8K: Grade school math
- BIG-Bench: Diverse tasks beyond current capabilities

Ethical Considerations

8K: Grade school math
- BIG-Bench: Diverse tasks beyond current capabilities

Ethical Considerations

LLMs raise several ethical concerns:
- Bias and fairness: Models can perpetuate and amplify societal biases
- Misinformation: Can be used to generate convincing fake content
- Privacy: Training data may contain private information
- Environmental impact: Training requires significant energy
- Job displacement: May automate human tasks
- Copyright: Trained on copyrighted material

ant energy
- Job displacement: May automate human tasks
- Copyright: Trained on copyrighted material

Responsible AI development for LLMs includes safety research, red teaming, and alignment techniques to ensure models are helpful, harmless, and honest.

Machine Learning

Machine learning (ML) is a field of study in artificial intelligence concerned with the development and study of statistical algorithms that can learn from data and generalize to unseen data, and thus perform tasks without explicit instructions. Recently, artificial neural networks have been able to surpass many previous approaches in performance.

ently, artificial neural networks have been able to surpass many previous approaches in performance.

ML finds application in many fields, including natural language processing, computer vision, speech recognition, email filtering, agriculture, and medicine. The application of ML to business problems is known as predictive analytics.

iculture, and medicine. The application of ML to business problems is known as predictive analytics.

Statistics and mathematical optimization (mathematical programming) methods comprise the foundations of machine learning. Data mining is a related (parallel) field of study, focusing on exploratory data analysis (EDA) via unsupervised learning.

From a theoretical viewpoint, probably approximately correct (PAC) learning provides a framework for describing machine learning.

History

approximately correct (PAC) learning provides a framework for describing machine learning.

History

The term machine learning was coined in 1959 by Arthur Samuel, an IBM employee and pioneer in the field of computer gaming and artificial intelligence. The synonym self-teaching computers was also used in this time period.

and artificial intelligence. The synonym self-teaching computers was also used in this time period.

By the early 1960s an experimental "learning machine" with punched tape memory, called Cybertron, had been developed by Raytheon Company to analyze sonar signals, electroencephalograms, and electrocardiograms.

veloped by Raytheon Company to analyze sonar signals, electroencephalograms, and electrocardiograms. The program named SAINT, developed by James Slagle at MIT in 1963, was capable of solving freshman-level symbolic integration problems originally considered to require human intelligence.

ng freshman-level symbolic integration problems originally considered to require human intelligence.

In 1967, the nearest neighbor algorithm was written, which is the foundation of basic pattern recognition. The algorithm was used to map routes and was one of the first algorithms to tackle the famous traveling salesman problem.

d to map routes and was one of the first algorithms to tackle the famous traveling salesman problem.

In 1970, Seppo Linnainmaa published the general method for automatic differentiation (AD) of discrete connected networks of nested differentiable functions. This is the technique known as backpropagation, which is used to train deep learning models.

In 1979, students at Stanford University invented the "Stanford Cart" which could navigate a chair-filled room on its own.

tanford University invented the "Stanford Cart" which could navigate a chair-filled room on its own.

In the 1980s and 1990s, the field experienced a major paradigm shift away from rule-based systems toward data-driven approaches. Support vector machines (SVMs) were introduced in 1992 by Boser, Guyon, and Vapnik.

iven approaches. Support vector machines (SVMs) were introduced in 1992 by Boser, Guyon, and Vapnik.

In 2009, the ImageNet dataset was released, enabling deep learning research to flourish. In 2012, the deep convolutional neural network AlexNet won the ImageNet competition by a large margin, marking the beginning of the deep learning era.

Types of Machine Learning

Supervised learning

gin, marking the beginning of the deep learning era.

Types of Machine Learning

Supervised learning

Supervised learning algorithms build a mathematical model of a set of data that contains both the inputs and the desired outputs. The data, known as training data, consists of a set of training examples. Each training example has one or more inputs and the desired output, also known as a supervisory signal.

training example has one or more inputs and the desired output, also known as a supervisory signal. In the mathematical model, each training example is represented by an array or vector, sometimes called a feature vector, and the training data is represented by a matrix. Through iterative optimization of an objective function, supervised learning algorithms learn a function that can be used to predict the output associated with new inputs.

rning algorithms learn a function that can be used to predict the output associated with new inputs.

Examples of supervised learning algorithms include linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, and neural networks.

Unsupervised learning

decision trees, random forests, support vector machines, and neural networks.

Unsupervised learning

Unsupervised learning algorithms find structures in data that has not been labeled, classified or categorized. Instead of responding to feedback, unsupervised learning algorithms identify commonalities in the data and react based on the presence or absence of such commonalities in each new piece of data.

the data and react based on the presence or absence of such commonalities in each new piece of data.

Common examples of unsupervised learning algorithms include k-means clustering, DBSCAN, hierarchical clustering, and autoencoders. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is also an unsupervised technique used for dimensionality reduction.

Reinforcement learning

s (PCA) is also an unsupervised technique used for dimensionality reduction.

Reinforcement learning

Reinforcement learning (RL) is an area of machine learning concerned with how intelligent agents ought to take actions in an environment in order to maximize the notion of cumulative reward. Reinforcement learning is one of three basic machine learning paradigms, alongside supervised learning and unsupervised learning.

of three basic machine learning paradigms, alongside supervised learning and unsupervised learning.

In RL, an agent interacts with an environment and learns through trial and error. The agent receives rewards for desirable actions and penalties for undesirable ones. Famous applications include DeepMind's AlphaGo, which defeated world champion Go players, and OpenAI's work on robotic manipulation.

Semi-supervised learning

ated world champion Go players, and OpenAI's work on robotic manipulation.

Semi-supervised learning

Semi-supervised learning falls between unsupervised learning (without any labeled training data) and supervised learning (with completely labeled training data). It makes use of a small amount of labeled data and a large amount of unlabeled data during training. This approach is useful when labeling data is expensive or time-consuming.

Self-supervised learning

This approach is useful when labeling data is expensive or time-consuming.

Self-supervised learning

Self-supervised learning is a type of unsupervised learning where the system learns to predict part of its input from other parts. This includes methods like masked language modeling used in BERT and next-token prediction used in GPT models.

Key Concepts

Feature engineering

deling used in BERT and next-token prediction used in GPT models.

Key Concepts

Feature engineering

Feature engineering is the process of using domain knowledge to extract features from raw data via data mining techniques. These features can be used to improve the performance of machine learning algorithms. Feature engineering can be very tedious and relies heavily on domain expertise and intuition.

Overfitting and underfitting

be very tedious and relies heavily on domain expertise and intuition.

Overfitting and underfitting

Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well, including its noise and random fluctuations. An overfit model performs well on training data but poorly on unseen test data. Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data.

data. Underfitting occurs when a model is too simple to capture the underlying patterns in the data.

To combat overfitting, regularization techniques such as L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) regularization are commonly used. Cross-validation is another important technique for assessing model generalization.

Bias-variance tradeoff

alidation is another important technique for assessing model generalization.

Bias-variance tradeoff

The bias-variance tradeoff is a central problem in supervised learning. Ideally, one wants to choose a model that both accurately captures the regularities in its training data, but also generalizes well to unseen data. Unfortunately, it is typically impossible to do both simultaneously.

Applications

l to unseen data. Unfortunately, it is typically impossible to do both simultaneously.

Applications

Machine learning has numerous applications across various industries:
- Healthcare: Disease diagnosis, drug discovery, personalized medicine
- Finance: Fraud detection, algorithmic trading, credit scoring
- Transportation: Self-driving cars, route optimization, traffic prediction
- Natural language processing: Machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots
- Computer vision: Image classificat

e processing: Machine translation, sentiment analysis, chatbots
- Computer vision: Image classification, object detection, facial recognition
- Recommendation systems: Netflix, Amazon, Spotify recommendations

Artificial Neural Networks

An artificial neural network (ANN) is a computational model inspired by the structure and functioning of biological neural networks in the brain. ANNs are a subset of machine learning and form the backbone of deep learning algorithms.

the brain. ANNs are a subset of machine learning and form the backbone of deep learning algorithms.

The first practical ANN was the perceptron, invented by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958. The modern deep learning renaissance began after 2012 when AlexNet demonstrated dramatic improvements in image recognition.

Structure of Neural Networks

Neurons and Layers

trated dramatic improvements in image recognition.

Structure of Neural Networks

Neurons and Layers

A neural network is composed of layers of interconnected nodes or "neurons." Each connection can transmit a signal to other neurons. An artificial neuron receives signals, processes them, and can signal neurons connected to it.

rons. An artificial neuron receives signals, processes them, and can signal neurons connected to it.

The typical structure includes:
- Input layer: Receives raw feature data
- Hidden layers: One or more intermediate layers that learn representations
- Output layer: Produces the final prediction or output

Each neuron in a layer is typically connected to every neuron in the following layer (fully connected or dense layer).

Weights and Biases

nnected to every neuron in the following layer (fully connected or dense layer).

Weights and Biases

Each connection between neurons has an associated weight, which determines the strength of the connection. Each neuron also has a bias term. Learning in neural networks involves adjusting these weights and biases to minimize a loss function.

The output of a neuron is calculated as:
output = activation_function(sum(weights * inputs) + bias)

Activation Functions

n is calculated as:
output = activation_function(sum(weights * inputs) + bias)

Activation Functions

Activation functions introduce non-linearity into the network, allowing it to learn complex patterns:

1. Sigmoid: σ(x) = 1/(1+e^(-x))
   - Output range: (0, 1)
   - Used for binary classification output
   - Suffers from vanishing gradient for deep networks

1)
   - Used for binary classification output
   - Suffers from vanishing gradient for deep networks

2. Hyperbolic Tangent (tanh): tanh(x) = (e^x - e^(-x))/(e^x + e^(-x))
   - Output range: (-1, 1)
   - Zero-centered, better than sigmoid for hidden layers

3. Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU): f(x) = max(0, x)
   - Most popular activation for hidden layers
   - Computationally efficient
   - Can suffer from "dying ReLU" problem

4. Leaky ReLU: f(x) = max(0.01x, x)
   - Addresses dying ReLU problem

fer from "dying ReLU" problem

4. Leaky ReLU: f(x) = max(0.01x, x)
   - Addresses dying ReLU problem

5. Softmax: Converts logits to probability distribution
   - Used for multi-class classification output layer

Training Neural Networks

Forward Propagation

- Used for multi-class classification output layer

Training Neural Networks

Forward Propagation

During forward propagation, input data flows through the network layer by layer. At each layer, the weighted sum of inputs is computed and passed through an activation function. The final output is compared to the target using a loss function.

Loss Functions

tivation function. The final output is compared to the target using a loss function.

Loss Functions

Loss functions measure how well the network's predictions match the true labels:
- Mean Squared Error (MSE): Used for regression tasks
- Binary Cross-Entropy: Used for binary classification
- Categorical Cross-Entropy: Used for multi-class classification
- Huber Loss: Robust to outliers

Backpropagation

Cross-Entropy: Used for multi-class classification
- Huber Loss: Robust to outliers

Backpropagation

Backpropagation is the key algorithm for training neural networks. It works by computing the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight using the chain rule of calculus, then updating weights in the direction that reduces loss.

h weight using the chain rule of calculus, then updating weights in the direction that reduces loss.

The algorithm:
1. Perform forward pass to compute output and loss
2. Compute gradient of loss at output
3. Propagate gradients backward through the network
4. Update weights using gradient descent

Gradient Descent Variants

nts backward through the network
4. Update weights using gradient descent

Gradient Descent Variants

- Batch Gradient Descent: Computes gradient on entire dataset
- Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD): Updates after each sample
- Mini-batch Gradient Descent: Updates using small batches
- Adam Optimizer: Adaptive learning rates with momentum
- AdaGrad: Adapts learning rate per parameter
- RMSprop: Maintains moving average of squared gradients

Hyperparameters

earning rate per parameter
- RMSprop: Maintains moving average of squared gradients

Hyperparameters

Key hyperparameters in neural network training:
- Learning rate: Controls step size during optimization
- Batch size: Number of training examples per update
- Number of epochs: How many times the full dataset is passed
- Network architecture: Number and size of layers
- Regularization parameters: Dropout rate, weight decay

Regularization Techniques

Dropout

f layers
- Regularization parameters: Dropout rate, weight decay

Regularization Techniques

Dropout

Dropout is a regularization technique where randomly selected neurons are ignored during training. This prevents complex co-adaptations and forces the network to learn more robust features. Typical dropout rates are 0.2 to 0.5.

Batch Normalization

he network to learn more robust features. Typical dropout rates are 0.2 to 0.5.

Batch Normalization

Batch normalization normalizes the inputs to each layer, reducing internal covariate shift. It allows higher learning rates, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and acts as a form of regularization.

Early Stopping

Early stopping monitors performance on a validation set during training and stops when validation performance begins to degrade, preventing overfitting.

set during training and stops when validation performance begins to degrade, preventing overfitting.

Types of Neural Networks

Feedforward Networks (Multi-Layer Perceptrons)

The simplest type of neural network where information moves only in one direction—forward—from input nodes, through hidden nodes to output nodes. There are no cycles or loops.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

gh hidden nodes to output nodes. There are no cycles or loops.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)

Specialized for processing grid-like data (images). Use convolutional layers with shared weights to detect local features. Key operations: convolution, pooling, and fully connected classification.

Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

rations: convolution, pooling, and fully connected classification.

Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

Designed for sequential data. Connections form directed cycles, allowing information to persist. Variants include LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) and GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit).

Transformer Networks

Use self-attention mechanisms rather than recurrence. Highly parallelizable and effective for NLP tasks. The dominant architecture in modern large language models.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)

NLP tasks. The dominant architecture in modern large language models.

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs)

Operate on graph-structured data. Used for social network analysis, molecular property prediction, and knowledge graphs.

Universal Approximation Theorem

work analysis, molecular property prediction, and knowledge graphs.

Universal Approximation Theorem

The universal approximation theorem states that a feedforward network with a single hidden layer containing a finite number of neurons can approximate continuous functions on compact subsets of R^n, under mild assumptions on the activation function.

e continuous functions on compact subsets of R^n, under mild assumptions on the activation function.

This theorem provides theoretical justification for why neural networks are powerful function approximators. However, it does not say anything about the learnability of these functions.

Natural Language Processing

Natural language processing (NLP) is an interdisciplinary subfield of computer science and artificial intelligence.

e processing (NLP) is an interdisciplinary subfield of computer science and artificial intelligence. It is primarily concerned with providing computers with the ability to process data encoded in natural language and is thus closely related to information retrieval, knowledge representation and computational linguistics, a subfield of linguistics.

mation retrieval, knowledge representation and computational linguistics, a subfield of linguistics.

Typically, data is collected in text corpora, using either rule-based, statistical, or neural-based approaches to build systems that perform tasks such as speech tagging, parsing, named entity recognition, sentiment analysis, language translation, and question answering.

History

named entity recognition, sentiment analysis, language translation, and question answering.

History

The history of natural language processing generally started in the 1950s, although work can be found from earlier periods. In 1950, Alan Turing published an article titled "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" which proposed what is now called the Turing test as a criterion of intelligence, a task that involves the automated interpretation and generation of natural language.

intelligence, a task that involves the automated interpretation and generation of natural language.

The Georgetown–IBM experiment in 1954 involved fully automatic translation of more than sixty Russian sentences into English. The authors claimed that within three to five years, machine translation would be a solved problem.

The authors claimed that within three to five years, machine translation would be a solved problem. However, real progress was much slower, and after the ALPAC report in 1966, which found that ten-year-long research had failed to fulfill the expectations, funding for machine translation was dramatically reduced.

ch had failed to fulfill the expectations, funding for machine translation was dramatically reduced.

The 1980s and 1990s saw a revolution in NLP with the introduction of machine learning algorithms for language processing. This was due in part to the steady increase in computational power and the gradual lessening of the dominance of Chomskyan theories of linguistics, in which the statistical approach to NLP was marginalised.

nce of Chomskyan theories of linguistics, in which the statistical approach to NLP was marginalised.

In the 2010s, representation learning and deep neural networks became common in NLP research. Neural machine translation emerged as a major improvement over statistical approaches. Word embeddings (word2vec) were introduced in 2013 by Mikolov et al. at Google.

Key NLP Tasks

Text Classification

(word2vec) were introduced in 2013 by Mikolov et al. at Google.

Key NLP Tasks

Text Classification

Text classification is the task of assigning predefined categories to text. Applications include:
- Spam detection
- Sentiment analysis (positive/negative/neutral)
- Topic categorization
- Language identification
- Intent detection in chatbots

Named Entity Recognition (NER)

egorization
- Language identification
- Intent detection in chatbots

Named Entity Recognition (NER)

Named entity recognition (NER) seeks to locate and classify named entities mentioned in unstructured text into pre-defined categories such as person names, organizations, locations, medical codes, time expressions, quantities, monetary values, percentages, etc.

Machine Translation

medical codes, time expressions, quantities, monetary values, percentages, etc.

Machine Translation

Machine translation is the task of automatically translating text from one language to another. Early approaches used rule-based systems. Statistical machine translation became dominant in the 2000s. Neural machine translation using encoder-decoder architectures became state-of-the-art in the 2010s.

Question Answering

lation using encoder-decoder architectures became state-of-the-art in the 2010s.

Question Answering

Question answering (QA) is a computer science discipline within the fields of information retrieval and NLP that is concerned with building systems that automatically answer questions posed by humans in a natural language.

ned with building systems that automatically answer questions posed by humans in a natural language.

Types of QA systems include:
- Closed-domain: Specialized in a specific domain (e.g., medical or legal)
- Open-domain: Can answer questions about any topic
- Reading comprehension: Answers based on a given passage
- Knowledge-base QA: Uses structured knowledge bases

Sentiment Analysis

rs based on a given passage
- Knowledge-base QA: Uses structured knowledge bases

Sentiment Analysis

Sentiment analysis or opinion mining is the use of NLP to identify and extract subjective information from text, especially opinions, attitudes, and emotions. Applications include brand monitoring, product review analysis, political opinion tracking, and customer feedback analysis.

Text Summarization

uct review analysis, political opinion tracking, and customer feedback analysis.

Text Summarization

Text summarization is the task of producing a concise and fluent summary while preserving key information and overall meaning. Approaches include:
- Extractive summarization: Selects key sentences from the source text
- Abstractive summarization: Generates new text that captures the meaning

Language Models and Embeddings

Word Embeddings

ation: Generates new text that captures the meaning

Language Models and Embeddings

Word Embeddings

Word embeddings are dense vector representations of words where semantically similar words map to nearby points in vector space. Key methods include:
- Word2Vec (2013): Predicts surrounding words (Skip-gram) or predicts target word from context (CBOW)
- GloVe (2014): Uses global co-occurrence statistics
- FastText: Handles subword information, useful for morphologically rich languages

rrence statistics
- FastText: Handles subword information, useful for morphologically rich languages

Contextualized Embeddings

Unlike static word embeddings, contextualized embeddings change based on surrounding context:
- ELMo (2018): Uses bidirectional LSTM to generate context-sensitive representations
- BERT (2018): Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, trained with masked language modeling
- GPT (2018): Generative Pre-trained Transformer, trained with language modeling

d language modeling
- GPT (2018): Generative Pre-trained Transformer, trained with language modeling

Large Language Models

Large language models (LLMs) are language models consisting of a neural network with many parameters (typically billions of weights), trained on large quantities of unlabeled text. LLMs emerged around 2018 and perform well at a wide variety of tasks.

quantities of unlabeled text. LLMs emerged around 2018 and perform well at a wide variety of tasks.

Notable LLMs include:
- GPT-3 and GPT-4 (OpenAI): Used for various generation tasks
- BERT and its variants (Google): Used for understanding tasks
- T5 (Google): Text-to-text transfer transformer
- LLaMA (Meta): Open-source large language model
- Claude (Anthropic): Constitutional AI-based assistant

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

model
- Claude (Anthropic): Constitutional AI-based assistant

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the parametric knowledge stored in model weights, RAG systems:
1. Retrieve relevant documents from a knowledge base using the user's query
2. Pass the retrieved documents as context to an LLM
3. Generate an answer grounded in the retrieved information

retrieved documents as context to an LLM
3. Generate an answer grounded in the retrieved information

RAG was introduced by Lewis et al. at Facebook AI Research in 2020. It addresses the problem of hallucination in LLMs by grounding responses in real documents. RAG systems are particularly useful for domain-specific question answering, where the LLM may not have sufficient training data.

useful for domain-specific question answering, where the LLM may not have sufficient training data.

Key components of a RAG system:
- Document store/vector database (FAISS, Chroma, Pinecone)
- Embedding model to encode documents and queries
- Retriever to find relevant documents
- Generator LLM to produce final answers

NLP Evaluation Metrics

BLEU Score

find relevant documents
- Generator LLM to produce final answers

NLP Evaluation Metrics

BLEU Score

BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) is an algorithm for evaluating the quality of text which has been machine-translated from one natural language to another. It measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference texts.

ROUGE Score

language to another. It measures n-gram overlap between generated and reference texts.

ROUGE Score

ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) is a set of metrics used for evaluating automatic summarization and machine translation. ROUGE-N measures n-gram recall, ROUGE-L measures longest common subsequence.

Perplexity

ranslation. ROUGE-N measures n-gram recall, ROUGE-L measures longest common subsequence.

Perplexity

Perplexity is a measurement of how well a probability distribution or probability model predicts a sample. A lower perplexity score indicates better performance.

F1 Score

F1 score is commonly used for tasks like named entity recognition and question answering. It is the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
